INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.question: What does UML stand for in the context of Object-Oriented Programming?
options: Unified Modeling Language
Universal Machine Logic
User Module Layers
Underlying Memory Layout

INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.question: Which of the following is NOT a standard component of a UML class diagram?
options: Memory Addresses
Class Name
Attributes
Operations

INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.question: What happens when a Python program encounters an unexpected error and cannot handle it?
options: It raises an Exception and terminates
It skips the line and continues running
It automatically restarts the program
It compiles the code into machine language
INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.answer: It raises an Exception and terminates

INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.question: Which keyword is used in Python to manually throw an exception if a specific condition occurs?
options: raise
throw
catch
except

INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.question: What is the specific purpose of a 'try-except' block in Python?
options: To execute a block of code and catch/handle exceptions if they occur, preventing a crash
To test if a variable is True or False
To loop through a list until an error is found
To import external libraries safely
INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.answer: To execute a block of code and catch/handle exceptions if they occur, preventing a crash

INFO135 - OOP & Exceptions.question: Which built-in Exception is raised when a number is attempted to be divided by zero?
options: ZeroDivisionError
ArithmeticError
FloatingPointError
ValueError

INFO135 - Trees & Heaps.question: What is the defining rule of a Max-heap structure?
options: For each non-leaf node, the node's value is greater than the value of its children
For each non-leaf node, the node's value is smaller than the value of its children
The root node must always be exactly double the value of its leaves
Every node can only have one child
INFO135 - Trees & Heaps.answer: For each non-leaf node, the node's value is greater than the value of its children

INFO135 - Trees & Heaps.question: In a Min-heap, where is the absolute smallest element located?
options: At the root
At the deepest leaf node
In the rightmost child node
In the exact middle of the tree

INFO135 - Trees & Heaps.question: Is a Heap considered a linear or non-linear data structure?
options: Non-linear data structure
Linear data structure
Both, depending on the programming language
Neither, it is an Abstract Data Type only

INFO135 - Trees & Heaps.question: What is the main structural difference between a linear and a non-linear data structure?
options: Linear elements are attached sequentially; non-linear elements are attached hierarchically
Linear elements can only hold text; non-linear elements hold numbers
Linear data structures require less memory than non-linear ones
Linear structures can only be sorted using Bubble Sort
INFO135 - Trees & Heaps.answer: Linear elements are attached sequentially; non-linear elements are attached hierarchically

INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.question: How does Dijkstra's algorithm differ from a basic Breadth-First Search (BFS)?
options: Dijkstra takes edge weights (costs/time) into account, whereas BFS only counts the number of nodes/edges
BFS is used for shortest paths, while Dijkstra is used for sorting arrays
Dijkstra only works on linear data structures
BFS always finds the fastest route in terms of time, regardless of the number of nodes
INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.answer: Dijkstra takes edge weights (costs/time) into account, whereas BFS only counts the number of nodes/edges

INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.question: What is the core philosophy of a Greedy Algorithm?
options: Making the best local choice at the moment with the hope of finding a global optimum
Storing all previous solutions in memory to avoid repeating calculations
Dividing the problem into exactly two halves and solving them recursively
Randomly guessing paths until the correct one is found
INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.answer: Making the best local choice at the moment with the hope of finding a global optimum

INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.question: When comparing Dynamic Programming to Greedy Algorithms, which statement is generally true?
options: Dynamic programming finds the optimal solution but is slower, while Greedy does not always find the optimal solution but is fast
Greedy algorithms always find the optimal solution, while DP is just an estimation
Dynamic programming uses less memory than Greedy Algorithms
They are the exact same algorithm implemented in different languages
INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.answer: Dynamic programming finds the optimal solution but is slower, while Greedy does not always find the optimal solution but is fast

INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.question: Why do we store sub-solutions in Dynamic Programming (Memoization)?
options: To avoid repeating the same calculations for overlapping subproblems
Because Python requires all variables to be stored globally
To easily print the output to the console later
To increase the worst-case time complexity intentionally
INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.answer: To avoid repeating the same calculations for overlapping subproblems

INFO135 - Advanced Algorithms.question: If you solve the Knapsack problem using pure recursion without Dynamic Programming, how does the number of subproblems grow?
options: Exponentially (e.g., 2^n)
Linearly (O(n))
Logarithmically (O(log n))
Constant time (O(1))

INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.question: In the context of Hashing, what formula is typically used to represent Linear Probing to find an empty slot?
options: slot = (home + i) % m
slot = (home * i) / m
slot = home + (i % m)
slot = m % (home + i)

INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.question: If a hash table uses linear probing, what exactly happens when a 'collision' occurs?
options: The algorithm loops sequentially to check the next available slots until an empty one is found
The program crashes and raises a KeyError exception
The new item completely overwrites the old item
The table size is immediately doubled
INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.answer: The algorithm loops sequentially to check the next available slots until an empty one is found

INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.question: What does an Abstract Data Type (ADT) define?
options: A well-defined description that specifies a set of operations performed on data, independent of implementation
The exact memory layout required for a list in Python
A physical piece of hardware used to store information
An algorithm for sorting strings
INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.answer: A well-defined description that specifies a set of operations performed on data, independent of implementation

INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.question: In a basic Singly Linked List, what does it mean if a Node's 'next' pointer evaluates to None?
options: It indicates the end (tail) of the linked list
It means the list is completely empty
It means the Node has been deleted
It indicates the head of the list
INFO135 - Hashing & Data Structures.answer: It indicates the end (tail) of the linked list

INFO135 - Additional Exam Examples.question: Exam Formula Check: If you are searching for a name in an UNSORTED phone directory with 'n' entries using Linear Search, what is the expected (average) number of entries checked?
options: log(n)

INFO135 - Additional Exam Examples.question: In the example exam question regarding the 'House' OOP class, what does the 'renovate(expense, new_condition)' method do?
options: It increases the cost by the expense and updates the condition of the house
It sells the house and calculates the final profit
It deletes the house object from memory
It changes the owner's name and resets the price to zero
INFO135 - Additional Exam Examples.answer: It increases the cost by the expense and updates the condition of the house

INFO135 - Additional Exam Examples.question: Exam Theory: What causes a process to be defined as 'I/O-Bound'?
options: The program spends most of its time waiting for input/output operations (like network requests or reading files) to complete
The CPU is too slow to handle the mathematical calculations
The program has entered an infinite while loop
The user has not pressed a key on the keyboard fast enough
INFO135 - Additional Exam Examples.answer: The program spends most of its time waiting for input/output operations (like network requests or reading files) to complete

INFO135 - Exam Examples.question: Suppose you're looking for a word in a Spanish dictionary with 262144 words. In the worst case, how many steps do you think the search will take with Binary Search? [cite: 3223]
options: 2^18 step
2 step
18 step
64 step
None of the above

INFO135 - Exam Examples.question: Sara's mother gave her a phone number and asked her to check a printed phone directory and find the name of the person who owns the number. Suppose Sara uses Linear search. If there are n entries in the phone directory, how many entries is 'expected' to be checked by Sara (average case), before finding the name of the owner of that number? [cite: 3223]
options: n
log n

INFO135 - Exam Examples.question: What does I/O-Bound mean? [cite: 3223]
options: A condition where a program slows down because it must wait for input/output (I/O) operations to be completed
A condition where the CPU runs at maximum capacity
A condition where a program uses too much memory
A condition where the network is disconnected
INFO135 - Exam Examples.answer: A condition where a program slows down because it must wait for input/output (I/O) operations to be completed
